Excellent findings! Let me analyze:

  1. CPU: Kunpeng 920, gcc -march=native gives armv8.2-a+crypto+dotprod+fp16fml
  2. GCC march default: armv8-a (generic), -mcpu=generic, -mtune=generic
  3. Source exists: /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25 and several build directories exist
  4. OS: openEuler 22.03 LTS-SP2
  5. LSE instructions: 12,664 matches found for cas/swp/ldadd patterns - these are PLT entries, not actual LSE instructions. The objdump grep found PLT symbols containing "cast" patterns, not actual LSE atomics
  6. No outline-atomics found in the binary - the default GCC 10.3 uses -moutline-atomics but only for GCC 10+, and the binary seems to have been compiled WITHOUT this
  7. GCC default march is armv8-a - but the CPU supports armv8.2-a with crypto, dotprod, fp16fml
  8. No explicit compilation flags visible in the binary strings - the build was done with default GCC flags

Let me check the actual build directories to find CMakeCache and compilation flags.

🔗 Sub-assistant

Excellent findings! The source exists, and I found critical compilation gaps. Let me check the build directories for actual compilation flags.

2026-06-26 02:45:07
de7bdebe-c76 → fec80b83-5c7
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== List build directories ===';
for d in /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-official-arm-crc-lse /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-opt /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-lse-crc; do
  echo '--- $d ---';
  ls -la \$d/ 2>&1 | head -10;
  if [ -f \$d/CMakeCache.txt ]; then
    echo '>>> CMakeCache.txt found in \$d';
    grep -E 'CMAKE_C_FLAGS|CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS|CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE|WITH_|MARCH|MCPU|MTUNE|LTO|DCMAKE_' \$d/CMakeCache.txt 2>&1;
  fi
  if [ -f \$d/Makefile ]; then
    echo '>>> Makefile found in \$d';
  fi
done
echo '=== Source dir check ===';
ls -la /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/ | head -10;
"

2026-06-26 02:45:10
fe73dea9-106 → fb0ab980-b8a
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== Find CMakeCache in all dirs ===';
find /home/mysql -name 'CMakeCache.txt' 2>/dev/null;
echo '';
for f in \$(find /home/mysql -name 'CMakeCache.txt' 2>/dev/null); do
  echo '=== \$f ===';
  grep -E 'CMAKE_C_FLAGS|CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS|CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE|DCMAKE_INSTALL|WITH_|CMAKE_C_COMPILER|CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER|CMAKE_LINKER|ENABLED_PROFILING|ENABLED_LOCAL_INFILE|MYSQL_DATADIR|SYSCONFDIR|WITH_BOOST|DOWNLOAD_BOOST|WITH_UNIT_TESTS|WITH_DEBUG|WITH_ASAN|WITH_TSAN|WITH_VALGRIND' \$f 2>&1;
  echo '---';
done
"

Wait, the CMakeCache.txt I need to focus on is the one used for the currently running MySQL binary. Let me check which build directory was used for the currently installed mysqld at /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld.

The key finding from the first scan:

Build directory: /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-official-arm-crc-lse/build-arm-crc-lse/ or /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/

Looking at CMakeCache.txt in /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/CMakeCache.txt:

CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE:STRING=Release
CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS:STRING=-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition
CMAKE_C_FLAGS:STRING=-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition

Wait, but the binary doesn't seem to have LSE instructions... Let me recheck. The build directory mysql-8.0.25-build-opt has LSE and all the flags, but is that the directory used for the currently installed binary? The binary date is Apr 23 14:55 at /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld.

And the CMakeCache in /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/CMakeCache.txt has:

  • -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse
  • -mtune=native
  • -flto=auto (LTO)
  • -O3
  • WITH_LTO:BOOL=OFF (this is MySQL's own LTO setting, different from GCC LTO)

But wait, WITH_LTO:BOOL=OFF means MySQL's LTO support is off, but the LTO flags are still in CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS. This is confusing. Let me think about this...

Actually, looking again, CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS has -flto=auto which means GCC itself will use LTO. The WITH_LTO is MySQL's internal flag for enabling its own LTO support infrastructure. They might be different.

But the binary from mysql-8.0.25-official-arm-crc-lse might be the one that's actually running, not the mysql-8.0.25-build-opt one.

Let me check which build directory corresponds to the installed binary. I need to find the binary that was actually installed to /usr/local/mysql-opt/.

done

Key findings:

  1. The installed binary came from mysql-8.0.25-build-opt - confirmed by install_manifest.txt pointing to /usr/local/mysql-opt/ and timestamp alignment (CMakeCache: Apr 23 14:47, binary: Apr 23 14:55).

  2. The build-opt CMakeCache flags include:

    CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS:STRING=-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition
CMAKE_C_FLAGS:STRING=-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition

  3. The binary is 62MB and NOT stripped - it still has debug symbols.

  4. The binary already has significant optimization applied:

    • -O3 (highest optimization level)
    • -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse (full ARMv8.2 with CRC, crypto, LSE extensions)
    • -mtune=native (tuned for Kunpeng 920)
    • -flto=auto (LTO enabled)
    • -ffat-lto-objects
    • -fuse-linker-plugin
    • -fno-semantic-interposition

  5. BUT no PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) - this is the missing piece.

  6. No jemalloc (WITH_JEMALLOC:BOOL=OFF) - but that's not a compiler optimization issue.

So the current binary already has all the architecture-specific flags and LTO. Let me verify that LSE is actually in the binary by checking the actual assembly. Also, let me verify the install manifest match.

Let me also check if there's a build tree available that we can use for PGO recompilation.

2026-06-26 02:46:11
277ca51c-f60 → 524b2145-e17
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== CHECK ACTUAL LSE INSTRUCTIONS IN mysqld ===';
# LSE atomic instructions have specific opcodes
# CAS: cas/casa/casl/casal
# SWP: swp/swpa/swpl/swpal
# LDADD: ldadd/ldadda/ldaddal/ldaddl
echo '--- LSE CAS instruction ---';
objdump -d /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld | grep -cE '\scas\s|\scasa\s|\scasl\s|\scasal\s|\sswp\s|\sswpa\s|\sswpl\s|\sswpal\s|\sldadd\s|\sldadda\s|\sldaddal\s|\sldaddl\s' 2>/dev/null;
echo '--- load/store exclusive (non-LSE) ---';
objdump -d /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld | grep -cE '\sldxr\s|\sstxr\s|\sldaxr\s|\sstlxr\s' 2>/dev/null;
echo '';
echo '=== Check if the installed binary actually has LSE ===';
# Try a specific function known to use atomics in MySQL
objdump -d /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld | grep -E '\scas\s' | head -10;
echo '---';
# Check for LSE LDADD (common in lock implementations)
objdump -d /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld | grep -E '\sldadd\s' | head -10;
echo '---';
# Check non-LSE atomics
objdump -d /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld | grep -E '\sldxr\s' | head -10;
" 2>&1

Critical findings!

  1. LSE instructions confirmed present: 2,695 CAS instructions found, plus LDADD instructions. This confirms the binary was compiled with -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse and LSE is actually being used.

  2. No load/store exclusive instructions (0 found) - the binary uses LSE atomics everywhere, which is excellent for multi-socket Kunpeng.

...

Critical findings!

  1. LSE instructions confirmed present: 2,695 CAS instructions found, plus LDADD instructions. This confirms the binary was compiled with -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse and LSE is actually being used.

  2. No load/store exclusive instructions (0 found) - the binary uses LSE atomics everywhere, which is excellent for multi-socket Kunpeng.

  3. So the compiler flags from mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/CMakeCache.txt are confirmed effective:

    • -O3 ✓
    • -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse ✓ (confirmed by LSE instructions, CRC32 functions)
    • -mtune=native ✓
    • -flto=auto - LTO is being used

Now, what's missing:

  1. PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) - Not used. This is the biggest opportunity.
  2. -fomit-frame-pointer - This is default at -O3, so probably already used.
  3. jemalloc - Not related to compiler optimization.

Let me now think about the optimization potential:

Current state:

  • GCC 10.3.1 on openEuler 22.03
  • Compiled with: -O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition
  • LSE instructions confirmed in binary
  • CRC32 functions present
  • 64-bit binary, not stripped
  • LTO enabled

Opportunities:

  1. PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) - GCC 10.3 supports -fprofile-generate and -fprofile-use. For MySQL, this would:

    • Generate profiling data from a representative workload run
    • Recompile using that data
    • Expected gain: 5-15% for database workloads
    • PGO would optimize branch prediction, function inlining, and basic block layout based on actual sysbench workload patterns

  2. -fprofile-partial-training - With GCC 10, this flag handles code paths not covered by training.

  3. -fomit-frame-pointer - Should already be on with -O3, but worth checking.

  4. -Wl,--gc-sections - Remove unused code sections, reduces I-cache pressure.

  5. BOLT (Binary Optimization and Layout Tool) - LLVM-based post-link optimizer. Available in GCC 10 but requires LLVM tools. Not installed.

  6. Linker optimization - Already using LTO. Could check if -Wl,--gc-sections is useful.

  7. Better CPU-specific tuning - Kunpeng 920 is tsv110, we could use -mcpu=tsv110 which might give slightly better tuning than -mtune=native.

Wait, let me reconsider. The problem statement says:

  • IPC: 0.90 (low)
  • L1 I-cache misses: 11.5B/10s (high)

The L1 I-cache misses suggest frontend bottleneck. This could be improved by:

  • PGO: better code layout, function splitting (hot/cold)
  • -fno-semantic-interposition is already used
  • Function reordering via PGO

Given that the current binary already has most compile optimizations applied, let me focus on evaluating PGO since that's the main missing piece.

Let me check if we can do PGO with the existing build infrastructure.

find /home/mysql -name '*.gcda' -o -name '*.gcno' 2>/dev/null | head -10;
echo '=== Check if build-opt build tree exists ===';

df -h /home/mysql 2>&1;
echo '=== Check number of CPU cores for parallel build ===';

echo '=== Check available memory ===';
free -h;
echo '=== Check if sysbench is available ===';
which sysbench 2>&1;
echo '=== Check if the source tree has Makefile for re-configure ===';
ls /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/CMakeLists.txt 2>&1;
echo '=== Check GCC 10 PGO support ===';
gcc -fprofile-generate --version 2>&1 | head -1;
"

a8f53dac-93b → 16bc4e01-470
135 lines

Excellent findings! Let me consolidate:

Current state assessment:

...

Excellent findings! Let me consolidate:

Current state assessment:

Already applied optimizations (from CMakeCache.txt):

  • -O3 ✓
  • -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse ✓ (LSE confirmed via objdump: 2,695 CAS instructions, LDADD found)
  • -mtune=native ✓
  • -flto=auto ✓ (LTO enabled)
  • -ffat-lto-objects ✓
  • -fuse-linker-plugin ✓
  • -fno-semantic-interposition ✓
  • Not stripped (debug symbols present, but no -g flag in the main flags)

Missing:

  1. PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) - NOT used. This is the biggest opportunity.
  2. No jemalloc - but this isn't a compiler issue.
  3. Binary is not stripped - 62MB vs potentially ~15MB stripped. Not critical for performance but reduces disk/memory.

Environment for PGO:

  • GCC 10.3.1 supports PGO ✓
  • Source tree intact at /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/ ✓
  • Build dir at /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/ has CMakeCache.txt ✓
  • But build tree object files are partial (sql/ dir only has 19 items, likely cleaned after install)
  • 128 CPU cores ✓
  • 376GB RAM ✓
  • 3.7TB free on /home/mysql ✓
  • sysbench available ✓
  • jemalloc available on the system ✓
  • MySQL already has PGO support infrastructure in CMakeLists.txt (profile-arcs, test-coverage)

PGO feasibility assessment:

To do PGO, we need to:

  1. Create a new build directory with -fprofile-generate added to C/CXX flags
  2. Build MySQL with profile instrumentation
  3. Run a representative workload (sysbench oltp_read_only) to generate profile data
  4. Recompile with -fprofile-use using the generated profile data

However, a full MySQL PGO build on 128 cores would still take significant time (10-30 minutes). But given:

  • approved_execute mode
  • 3.7TB free space
  • 128 cores and 376GB RAM
  • Source and build infrastructure available

This is feasible. But first, let me estimate the build time and check if the existing build dir can be reused or a fresh one needed.

Actually, let me reconsider. The build-opt build tree has the CMakeFiles directory but the sql/ directory only has 19 items, suggesting most of the object files are missing (cleaned after install). We'd need to do a full rebuild.

A full MySQL 8.0.25 build with 128 cores would be about 5-10 minutes.

Let me also consider whether PGO is worth it:

  • Current TPS: 11,618
  • IPC: 0.90 (low)
  • L1 I-cache misses: 11.5B/10s (high)
  • PGO typically improves branch prediction, code layout, and reduces I-cache misses
  • Expected gain: 5-15% for database workloads

Given the low IPC and high I-cache misses, PGO could help significantly by:

  1. Better function layout (hot code grouped together, cold code separated)
  2. Better branch prediction (based on actual profile data)
  3. Better inlining decisions
  4. Improved basic block layout

But wait - the current binary already has -flto=auto (LTO). LTO already does whole-program optimization and inlining. PGO adds profile information to guide these decisions better.

Actually, thinking more carefully: the current binary already has very aggressive flags. Let me reconsider whether PGO would help beyond what LTO + O3 already does.

With LTO, the compiler can see all translation units but still doesn't know which branches are likely, which functions are hot/cold, and which code paths are common. PGO provides this runtime profile data, enabling:

  • Better basic block layout based on actual execution counts
  • Function splitting: hot sections grouped together, cold sections separated
  • Switch statement optimization based on case frequency
  • Better loop unrolling decisions
  • Better inlining decisions (inline hot functions even across TUs, don't inline cold ones)

The low IPC (0.90) and high L1 I-cache misses (11.5B/10s) suggest the CPU is spending a lot of time on frontend stalls - the instruction cache is thrashing. PGO's function reordering and hot/cold splitting would directly address this.

Let me also consider if there are other lower-risk options before going all-in on PGO:

  1. -fomit-frame-pointer - already default at -O3 on aarch64
  2. -Wl,--gc-sections + -ffunction-sections - could remove unused code, reducing binary size and I-cache pressure. Risk: very low. Gain: small but positive.
  3. -Wl,--icf=safe - identical code folding (Gold or LLD linker). Can reduce binary size.
  4. Stripping the binary - reduces load time and disk usage. No runtime perf impact.
  5. Rebuild with newer GCC (12 is available in a separate directory!) - could provide better optimizations

Actually wait, there's /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-gcc12/CMakeCache.txt - this suggests there's already a GCC 12 build attempt. Let me check that.

Also, there's mysqld-lse-stripped in the home directory, suggesting a stripped version was made.

Let me think about the best approach:

Given:

  1. Current binary is well-optimized already (O3, LTO, arch flags, LSE)
  2. Main gap is PGO
  3. We have the source and build infrastructure
  4. Environment is approved_execute
  5. Potential gain is 5-15% from PGO

I should:

  1. Check the GCC 12 build that exists
  2. Consider PGO as the primary candidate
  3. Also consider the easier wins like strip + gc-sections

Let me also consider the time it would take. A full MySQL PGO cycle would be:

  1. cmake with -fprofile-generate (5 min)
  2. make -j128 (10-15 min build)
  3. Install (1 min)
  4. Start MySQL with instrumented binary (1 min)
  5. Run sysbench workload to collect profile (5-10 min for representative profile)
  6. Stop MySQL (1 min)
  7. cmake with -fprofile-use (5 min)
  8. make -j128 with profile data (10-15 min)
  9. Install final binary (1 min)
  10. Restart MySQL (1 min)
  11. Run sysbench A/B test (5 min)

Total: ~45-60 minutes. This is feasible but lengthy.

Actually, wait. Let me re-read the SKILL.md requirements:

如果编译环境完整（有源码和构建目录），可尝试重新编译

And:

由于 mode=approved_execute，如果编译环境就绪且预计收益 >3%，执行优化。

3% of 11,618 = ~348 TPS. PGO should give 5-15%, so we're well above the threshold.

Let me also consider if there are quicker wins. For example:

  • The binary is 62MB and not stripped. Stripping saves space but doesn't affect runtime performance significantly (affects disk I/O at startup and memory for debug sections).
  • Adding -Wl,--gc-sections and -ffunction-sections could reduce the binary size by removing dead code, potentially improving I-cache efficiency.

Let me proceed with PGO as the primary recommendation since it's the biggest remaining opportunity.

But first, let me check the GCC 12 build - it might already have PGO or better results.

dfd99fb8-c88 → 089b9b55-bd7
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== PGO build time estimation ===';
# Check how many source files need compiling
echo 'Source files count:';
find /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25 -name '*.cc' -o -name '*.c' | wc -l;
echo '=== Check MySQL build time from logs ===';
ls -la /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/CMakeFiles/CMakeOutput.log 2>&1;
wc -l /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/CMakeFiles/CMakeOutput.log 2>&1;
echo '=== Check if link time for mysqld is known ===';
# Check build log timestamps
ls -la --time-style=full-iso /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/sql/ 2>&1 | head -20;
echo '=== Verify the current running binary PID ===';
pidof mysqld;
echo '=== Check which build's binary is currently running ===';
cat /proc/\$(pidof mysqld)/exe > /dev/null && md5sum /proc/\$(pidof mysqld)/exe;
"

Very interesting findings!

  1. GCC 12 build exists at /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-gcc12/ with flags:

    CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS:STRING=-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crypto+lse+crc+dotprod -mcpu=tsv110 -mtune=tsv110 -moutline-atomics -DNDEBUG -fno-plt -fno-semantic-interposition -fvisibility-inlines-hidden
    • Uses -mcpu=tsv110 (Kunpeng 920 specific!)
    • Uses -moutline-atomics
    • Uses -fno-plt
    • Uses -fvisibility-inlines-hidden
    • But compiler is at /host/home/mysql/gcc-12/bin/g++ which is a Docker path prefix

  2. GCC 12 not found at standard path - the build used a specific gcc-12 installation that's not in PATH

  3. Several pre-built stripped binaries exist in /home/mysql:

    • mysqld-lse-stripped (54MB) - LSE enabled, stripped
    • mysqld-crc32-arm (54MB) - CRC32 ARM variant, stripped
    • mysqld-crc32-v2 (54MB) - CRC32 v2, stripped
    • mysqld-crc32-v3 (57MB) - CRC32 v3, stripped
    • mysqld-cl128 (57MB) - CL128 variant, stripped

  4. The build-opt sql/ directory has library archives (libsql_dd.a 78MB, libsql_gis.a 171MB, libsql_main.a 169MB) - these are the compiled object archives, NOT cleaned!

Wait, this means the build directory still has most of the compiled objects in the .a archive files. The build was not fully cleaned. This makes a PGO rebuild much faster - we only need to recompile with different flags.

Actually, wait. If we change compiler flags (adding -fprofile-generate), cmake will detect the changes and trigger a full rebuild. The archives won't help because they were compiled without -fprofile-generate.

Let me think about this differently. The key question is: can we do PGO with the available infrastructure?

Let me check:

  1. Is GCC 12 available and working?
  2. Can we do a quick PGO cycle?

Actually, let me reconsider the approach. Given:

  • The current binary is well-optimized (O3, LTO, arch flags)
  • LSE is confirmed working
  • IPC is 0.90
  • L1 I-cache misses are high (11.5B/10s)

The main remaining optimization from the compiler side is PGO, which would help with:

  1. Better code layout to reduce I-cache misses
  2. Better inlining decisions based on actual profile
  3. Better branch prediction

But there's one more thing I should check: the GCC 12 build might not have actually been installed. Let me check if GCC 12 is available on the system.

Also, I should check if we can use the existing GCC 10.3.1 for PGO rather than GCC 12, since GCC 10 is confirmed to be working.

Let me also check the current building-opt for whether a rebuild is feasible.

Actually, let me focus on the practical plan now. I should:

  1. Check if GCC 12 is actually installed somewhere
  2. If not, use GCC 10.3 for PGO
  3. Plan and execute the PGO build

But before executing a full PGO build (which takes 45-60 min), let me first assess all the optimization opportunities more comprehensively and make a clear recommendation.

Let me also check if -fno-plt and -fvisibility-inlines-hidden (from the GCC 12 build) would help.

-fno-plt - Generates calls that don't go through the PLT. This can improve performance by avoiding PLT indirection. On aarch64, this is equivalent to using -fno-plt with GCC. It's worth considering but the gain is usually small (<1%).

-fvisibility-inlines-hidden - Hides inline function symbols, reducing symbol table size. Small gain.

-mcpu=tsv110 - Specifically tuned for Kunpeng 920. This is more specific than -mtune=native which would detect the CPU. Actually, -mtune=native on a Kunpeng 920 should be equivalent to -mcpu=tsv110 or better. Let me verify.

OK let me take a pragmatic approach. I'll:

  1. Check GCC 12 availability
  2. Do a quick assessment of whether the compile can be optimized further
  3. Make a clear recommendation

Let me also think about what I should report. The SKILL.md requires specific output fields:

{
  "compiler_profile_mode": "none / pgo / autofdo / bolt / hybrid",
  "recommended_compiler": "gcc-10.3.1 / gcc-12 / clang",
  "recommended_arch_flags": "...",
  "arch_flag_gap": "...",
  "codegen_verification": "...",
  "profile_guided_candidates": [...],
  "source_change_candidates": [...],
  "blocked_source_candidates": [...],
  "binary_equivalence_check": {...},
  "confounded_binary_test": false,
  "candidate_actions": [...],
  "further_compiler_optimization_potential": "...",
  "next_round_candidate": "..."
}

Now, let me decide on my course of action:

  1. PGO with GCC 10.3 - most feasible option

    • GCC 10.3 fully supports PGO
    • Source and build infrastructure available
    • 128 cores, 376GB RAM

    • Estimated total cycle: 45-60 minutes
    • Expected gain: 5-15%

    • Could provide additional optimizations beyond PGO
    • Higher risk, longer timeline

    • -mcpu=tsv110 instead of -mtune=native (likely equivalent)
    • Strip the binary (already has equivalent stripped versions available)

I think the right approach is:

Actually, re-reading the user's request:

Since mode=approved_execute, if compilation environment is ready and expected gain >3%, execute optimization.

But before executing, I need to think about whether PGO is truly safe and beneficial:

PGO risks:

  1. Training workload misrepresentation - if the training workload doesn't match production, PGO can actually hurt performance
  2. Build failure - the profile-generate build might fail
  3. Profile-guided optimization might not yield the expected gains

PGO benefits (for this specific case):

  1. The low IPC (0.90) and high I-cache misses (11.5B/10s) indicate frontend bottleneck
  2. PGO reorganizes code layout to reduce I-cache misses
  3. The sysbench workload is well-defined and reproducible
  4. Expected gain: 5-15% based on industry experience with DB workloads

Given that the current workload is sysbench oltp_read_only, which is very well-defined and reproducible, PGO training would be very representative.

Let me now write the comprehensive result. I'll:

  1. Document all findings
  2. Provide a clear PGO candidate action
  3. Also note the other optimization opportunities

Great findings:

  1. GCC 12.2.1 is available at /opt/openEuler/gcc-toolset-12/root/usr/bin/gcc - this is important!
  2. Also at /host/home/mysql/gcc-12/bin/gcc (same version, GCC 12.2.1)
  3. GCC 10.3.1 is the system default at /usr/bin/gcc
  4. The build-opt binary and installed binary have the SAME hash (both BuildID d132a5cff58caa231d0bf22a252a224fddcb0b77) - they are the same binary. The md5sum difference I saw earlier was because the build-opt binary's md5sum was for /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/bin/mysqld which is different from /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld - wait, no, the hashes match: both are fba69ca6623918d36b0d6c9c52c152da. Let me re-read...

Actually, from the earlier output:

fba69ca6623918d36b0d6c9c52c152da  /usr/local/mysql-opt/bin/mysqld

And:

11056379a11f8dbad6f87dcba439e438  /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/bin/mysqld

Wait, the build-opt bin/mysqld has a DIFFERENT hash! But then from the latest output:

BuildID[sha1]=d132a5cff58caa231d0bf22a252a224fddcb0b77

Both have the same BuildID. This is confusing. The BuildID is a hash embedded in the binary at link time, while md5sum is of the file content. If they have the same BuildID but different md5sum, it could mean:

  • They're the same binary but one was modified (stripped, modified, etc.)
  • Or the md5sum was for a different file

Let me not overthink this - the running binary is confirmed with hash fba69ca6623918d36b0d6c9c52c152da.

Now, key finding: GCC 12.2.1 is available at /opt/openEuler/gcc-toolset-12/root/usr/bin/gcc. This is the openEuler GCC toolset.

But before going into a full PGO rebuild, I need to be strategic. Let me think about what to recommend:

Option A: Quick Wins (low risk, immediate)

  • Already exhausted most compiler flags (O3, LTO, arch flags)
  • -fno-plt small gain
  • Not much left for "quick wins"

Option B: PGO with GCC 10 (medium effort, 5-15% gain)

  • Use existing GCC 10.3
  • Make a new build dir with -fprofile-generate
  • Run sysbench for training
  • Rebuild with -fprofile-use
  • Expected gain: 5-15%

Option C: GCC 12 + PGO (higher effort, potentially more gain)

  • GCC 12 has better auto-vectorization, LTO improvements
  • More modern optimizer

Let me now think about the PGO execution plan:

PGO Build Plan:

Phase 1: Profile-generate build

cd /home/mysql
mkdir mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build
cd mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build
cmake ../mysql-8.0.25 \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS="-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data" \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data" \
  ...
make -j128
make install

Wait, but LTO and PGO together can be tricky. When using -fprofile-generate with LTO, the profile data generation might not work correctly because LTO changes the compilation model.

Let me think about this more carefully.

Actually, GCC's LTO and PGO can work together, but the approach is:

  1. Build with -fprofile-generate and -flto - the LTO will still work during the instrumentation build
  2. Run the workload to generate profile data
  3. Build with -fprofile-use and -flto to use the profile data

GCC 10 supports combining LTO and PGO. The profile data is collected during the instrumented run, and then used in the final LTO + profile-use build.

However, there's a practical consideration: when using -fprofile-generate with LTO, the compiled code is slower (due to instrumentation), and the profile-generate binary may not achieve the same throughput. This is normal - we care about the profile data, not the instrumented binary's performance.

Also, there's a consideration about -ffat-lto-objects - this creates "fat" LTO objects that contain both intermediate language and normal object code. This is useful for compatibility but increases build time and binary size. For PGO, we might want to omit this.

Let me also think about the PGO training workload. We should run a sysbench workload that is representative of the production workload. In this case, it's oltp_read_only with the same table sizes, thread count, and runtime as the benchmark.

  2. Assessment of optimization gaps
  3. PGO candidate action with detailed commands
  4. Additional optimization recommendations

Let me also think about whether to use GCC 10 or GCC 12:

GCC 10 pros:

  • Known to work (used for current build)
  • No ABI issues
  • Lower risk

GCC 12 pros:

  • Better optimizer, auto-vectorization
  • Better LTO support
  • More modern
  • Available via toolset

GCC 12 cons:

  • Previous GCC 12 build failed (might have been configuration issue)
  • Need to source toolset environment correctly
  • Possible ABI issues with existing objects

I think using GCC 10 for PGO is the safer choice for now. GCC 12 could be a separate optimization round.

The cmake reconfiguration works. The build system is working. But make -j1 of the mysqld target will take a very long time. Let me check if the build-opt directory still has all object files or if they need to be rebuilt.

Actually, from earlier, I saw that the build-opt sql/ directory has the .a archives (libsql_dd.a, libsql_gis.a, libsql_main.a) which contain the compiled objects. But the Makefile is using cmake which tracks dependencies, so if the flags haven't changed, it can do an incremental build.

However, for PGO, we need to change the compilation flags (add -fprofile-generate), which will trigger a full rebuild. And we should use a fresh build directory anyway.

Let me also check how long the previous build took. The timestamps in the sql/ directory show:

  • libsql_dd.a: Apr 23 14:50
  • libsql_gis.a: Apr 23 14:51
  • libsql_main.a: Apr 23 14:53
  • Total build from cmake config (14:47) to end (14:55): ~8 minutes

Actually, the cmake output.log was generated at 14:47, and the last archive was at 14:53, and install was at 14:55. So the build took about 6-8 minutes from cmake to final binary. With 128 cores, this is quite fast.

OK, I think I have enough information now to write the comprehensive analysis. Let me now create the result JSON file.

Actually, I realize I should also think about whether to actually execute PGO or just provide the analysis and plan. Let me re-read the instructions:

Since mode=approved_execute, if compilation environment is ready and expected gain >3%, execute optimization.

OK so I need to execute. But executing PGO means:

  1. Create a new build directory
  2. Configure with -fprofile-generate
  3. Build the instrumented binary (~8 min)
  4. Install and start the instrumented binary
  5. Run sysbench to gather profile data (~10 min)
  6. Stop MySQL
  7. Reconfigure with -fprofile-use
  8. Build the optimized binary (~8 min)
  9. Install and restart MySQL
  10. Run the benchmark to verify improvement

Total estimated time: 30-40 minutes. This is doable but will take significant time.

However, I should also consider the risk: there's no parallel sysbench client that was used for the original 11,618 TPS benchmark. The local sysbench got only 243 TPS with default settings. For a good PGO training, we need a representative workload with the same level of parallelism.

Let me check what the actual benchmark parameters were. Looking at the cpu-affinity result, it mentions:

  • TPS: 11,618
  • Sysbench oltp_read_only
  • The client is at 192.168.90.105 (but we can't SSH there)

For PGO training, we need to run a workload that exercises the same code paths. Running sysbench locally with lower thread counts will still exercise the InnoDB code paths, but with different parallelism patterns. This is still useful for PGO because:

  • The hot functions (buffer pool, log system, lock system, b-tree operations) are the same
  • The branch patterns within those functions are similar
  • LTO + PGO will optimize the same hot paths

So even a local sysbench run with reasonable parameters (e.g., 32 threads, 10 tables, 1M rows each, 60-second run) would provide good training data.

echo '=== Prepare PGO build dir ===';
mkdir -p /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build
cd /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build
echo '=== Configure with -fprofile-generate ===';
cmake ../mysql-8.0.25 \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS='-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data' \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS='-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data' \
  -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo \
  -DWITH_BOOST=/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/boost \
  -DWITH_NUMA=ON \
  -DWITH_JEMALLOC=OFF \
  -DWITH_SSL=system \
  -DWITH_ZLIB=bundled \
  -DWITH_LZ4=bundled \
  -DWITH_ZSTD=bundled \
  -DWITH_ARCHIVE_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \
  -DWITH_BLACKHOLE_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \
  -DWITH_FEDERATED_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \
  -DDOWNLOAD_BOOST=0 \
  -DENABLED_LOCAL_INFILE=ON \
  -DWITH_UNIT_TESTS=OFF \
  -DWITH_DEBUG=OFF \
  -DWITH_MYSQLX=ON \
  -DWITH_ROUTER=OFF 2>&1 | tail -10
" 2>&1

The cmake configuration succeeded. Now I need to build the instrumented binary. Let me start the build with make -j128.

But wait - I noticed something in the cmake output:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE: -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG

This shows that MySQL's cmake is APPENDING the default Release flags (-ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG) on top of our CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS. This means the final compile flags would be:

-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG

Wait, there's a potential issue here: the Release flags have -O2 which comes AFTER our -O3. In GCC, the last -O flag wins, so this would actually use -O2 instead of -O3!

Let me check the original build-opt CMakeCache.txt to see what flags it used. Looking at the earlier output:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE:STRING=-O3 -DNDEBUG

In the original build-opt, the CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE was -O3 -DNDEBUG. So the final flags after appending would be:

-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse ... -DNDEBUG -O3 -DNDEBUG

Wait no, the CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE is used as the base for Release builds, and CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS is appended (or prepended). Let me think about this more carefully.

In CMake, for the Release configuration:

  1. CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE is applied on top of CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS
  2. The order depends on how CMake generates the compile command: typically CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS first, then CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE, then target-specific flags

So the compile flags would be:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS (-O3 -march=... -fprofile-generate=...) + CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE (-O3 -DNDEBUG) + target-specific flags

Wait, but from the cmake output:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE: -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG

This is different from the original cmake where CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE was -O3 -DNDEBUG. The difference is because this is a different version of the cmake cache or the default value from CMake itself (not from MySQL's CMakeLists.txt).

Actually wait, I think this is the default CMake value for CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE, which includes -O3 -DNDEBUG by default. But the output shows -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG, which is different. Let me check if this came from MySQL's own CMakeLists.txt.

Let me check the MySQL CMakeLists.txt for how it sets the Release flags. But actually, this concern about O2 vs O3 is important. In the original build-opt, the CMakeCache.txt showed:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE:STRING=-O3 -DNDEBUG

But in my new cmake run, it shows -O2 in the Release flags. This might be because:

  1. I didn't set -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE explicitly
  2. CMake defaults depend on the version or platform

Actually, looking at the original build-opt CMakeCache, the CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE was explicitly set to -O3 -DNDEBUG, which means it was overridden from the default. In my new cmake configuration, I didn't set this, so it might have gotten the default value which includes -O2.

However, I did set -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS='-O3 ...', so the -O3 is still in the CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS. The CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE is appended after CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS. If CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE has -O2, it would override the -O3 from CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS.

To ensure -O3 is used, I should also set -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE='-O3 -DNDEBUG' or ensure the -O3 flag comes last. Actually, the safest approach is to set both CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE and CMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE to be empty or to '-O3 -DNDEBUG', and put all optimization flags in CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS/CMAKE_C_FLAGS.

Wait, but actually I see this from the cmake output:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE: -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG

Hmm, this contains -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections which the original build-opt cmake did NOT have. This suggests something different in the cmake configuration.

Let me actually look at what flags the original build-opt used more carefully. The CMakeCache.txt showed:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE:STRING=-O3 -DNDEBUG

So in the original build, CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE was -O3 -DNDEBUG. But in my new config, it seems to be -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG. This is likely because the MySQL version or cmake version sets different defaults.

Let me fix this by explicitly setting CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE to -O3 -DNDEBUG to match the original, and also ensure the PGO flags are in the base CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS.

Actually, thinking about this more: the way CMake works with flags is:

  1. CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS - base flags (applied to all build types)
  2. CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE - additional flags for Release build type

The compile command is roughly: g++ $CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS $CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE $<TARGET_PROPERTY:COMPILE_FLAGS>

So if CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS = -O3 -march=... -fprofile-generate=... and CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE = -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG, the final command would be:

g++ -O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O2 -DNDEBUG ...

Since -O2 comes after -O3, the effective optimization level is -O2, NOT -O3! This is a problem.

I need to either:

  1. Remove -O3 from CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS and put it in CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE
  2. Or set CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE to empty and keep -O3 in CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS
  3. Or explicitly set CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE to -O3 -DNDEBUG

The cleanest approach is option 3: explicitly set both CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE and CMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE.

In the original build:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS: -O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition

...

Now I see the issue clearly.

In the original build:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS: -O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition
CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE: -O3 -DNDEBUG

So the final flags would be: -O3 -march=... -fno-semantic-interposition -O3 -DNDEBUG
The last -O3 wins, so it's properly -O3.

In my new build:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS: -O3 -march=... -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data
CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE: -O2 -DNDEBUG

Wait, CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE is -O2 -DNDEBUG. Since CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE is appended AFTER CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS, the final flags would be:
-O3 -march=... -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data -O2 -DNDEBUG

The last -O2 would override -O3, resulting in -O2 optimization level!

This is NOT what we want. I need to also set CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE and CMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE.

Let me look at what MySQL's compiler_options.cmake does. It says:

  • Line 247: STRING_APPEND(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE - this might be setting additional flags
  • Line 260: STRING_PREPEND(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE "${SECTIONS_FLAG} ") - this inserts -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections at the beginning

So MySQL's cmake modifies the Release flags based on conditions. The original build had these conditions evaluated, resulting in -O3 -DNDEBUG (no sections flags were added because the conditions were not met or they were added elsewhere).

Looking at lines 982-983, the -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections was added to CMAKE_C_FLAGS and CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS, not to the Release-specific flags.

Let me check what conditions control the section flags:

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/cmake/build_configurations/compiler_options.cmake:255:  STRING_PREPEND(CMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE          "${SECTIONS_FLAG} ")

The SECTIONS_FLAG would be -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections on GCC. But in the original build, this wasn't used because CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE was -O3 -DNDEBUG without the sections flags.

Let me look at how these are combined. Let me check the compiler_options.cmake file for the actual logic.

-DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE="-O3 -DNDEBUG"
-DCMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE="-O3 -DNDEBUG"

ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== Check MySQL compiler options for flag merging order ===';
grep -n 'CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE\|CMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE\|SECTIONS_FLAG\|STRING_PREPEND\|STRING_APPEND' /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/cmake/build_configurations/compiler_options.cmake | head -20;
echo '';
echo '=== Re-run cmake with proper Release flags ===';
rm -f /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/CMakeCache.txt
cd /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build && cmake ../mysql-8.0.25 \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS='-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data' \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS='-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data' \
  -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE='-O3 -DNDEBUG' \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE='-O3 -DNDEBUG' \
  -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo \
  -DWITH_BOOST=/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/boost \
  -DWITH_NUMA=ON \
  -DWITH_JEMALLOC=OFF \
  -DWITH_SSL=system \
  -DWITH_ZLIB=bundled \
  -DWITH_LZ4=bundled \
  -DWITH_ZSTD=bundled \
  -DWITH_ARCHIVE_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \

  -DWITH_FEDERATED_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \
  -DDOWNLOAD_BOOST=0 \
  -DENABLED_LOCAL_INFILE=ON \
  -DWITH_UNIT_TESTS=OFF \
  -DWITH_DEBUG=OFF \
  -DWITH_MYSQLX=ON \
  -DWITH_ROUTER=OFF 2>&1 | tail -5;
echo '';

grep -E 'CMAKE_C_FLAGS|CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS|CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE' /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/CMakeCache.txt | head -10;
" 2>&1

The build is failing because of a missing header file: mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h. This header file is typically generated by CMake during the configuration step. The fact that it's missing in the new build directory means the CMake configuration didn't generate it properly, or there's a path issue.

The original build-opt has this header generated. Let me check where it's located in the original build dir and copy it over, or re-generate it.

This is a known MySQL build issue. The psi_table_bits.h header is generated by CMake. Let me check if it's present in the original build-opt directory and copy it to the new build directory.

Wait, let me look more carefully. The include in handler.h says mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h. So the expected path is:

<include-dir>/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

But in the source tree, the file is at:

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h

This suggests that either:

  1. The build generates this header in the build directory with the bits/ subdirectory

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h

NOT in:

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

This is a source code issue in MySQL 8.0.25. The header file handler.h includes "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h" (with bits/), but the file actually exists at mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h (without bits/).

Wait, but the original build succeeded! So how did the original build-opt resolve this? Let me check...

The original build-opt's CMake probably had additional include paths that pointed to the include/mysql/components/services/ directory itself, so bits/psi_table_bits.h would resolve to include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h which doesn't exist. But in the original build, maybe the include path was set up such that the bits/ directory was in the include path?

Wait no. Let me re-read. The include says:

#include "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h"

This is a relative include. The compiler looks for:

  1. -I directories + mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h
  2. Or if there's a -I pointing to include/, then: include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

But the actual file is at include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h (without bits/).

Hmm, but the original build compiled successfully. So either:

  1. The include/ directory in the build-opt has a different structure (symlinks, copies)
  2. There's a generated file that creates the bits/ path
  3. Or the original build also had the same issue but it was resolved by copying or linking

Let me check if there's a bits/ directory inside the build-opt's include structure, and if that build-opt had a copy or symlink.

Actually, looking more carefully, the search for bits in the original build dir returned NO results. So the original build-opt directory doesn't have this header either!

This is confusing. How did the original build succeed then?

Wait - let me look at the cmake output from the original build again. The handler.h line 63 says:

#include "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h"

But the file is at:

/.../include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h

The key is: there's a bits/ subdirectory at include/mysql/components/services/bits/ that contains OTHER headers, but NOT psi_table_bits.h.

So for the original build to succeed, it must have had either:

  • A symlink or copy of psi_table_bits.h in the bits/ directory
  • Or an additional include path that resolved differently

Wait, maybe I'm overthinking this. Let me re-check the search. The search said:

=== Search broader in both build dirs ===
(no output)

But actually, the build-opt CMake might have generated additional include paths. Let me check the actual include paths used in the original build.

Actually, looking at the MySQL CMake output from the original build, it might have generated the mysql/components/services/bits/ directory containing psi_table_bits.h as part of the cmake configure step. Since I'm using a fresh build directory, this generated file might not have been created.

Let me check what generates this file by looking at the MySQL cmake scripts.

Wait, actually, I just realized the file IS in the source tree at:

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h

And the build needs it at:

<build-dir>/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

The <build-dir>/include/ is typically a copy or symlink of the source include/ directory. So the build dir's include path would be:

<build-dir>/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h

And when including mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h with -I<build-dir>/include, the compiler would look for:

<build-dir>/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

This path is WRONG because the file is at services/psi_table_bits.h not services/bits/psi_table_bits.h.

So the ORIGINAL build must have somehow created this file. Let me check if there's a cmake configure step that generates it.

Actually, you know what, let me just check if the bits directory exists in the original build-opt's include structure.

▼ 1 tool
💻 Bash Copy missing psi_table_bits.h to build dir and check for other missing headers
2026-06-26 03:02:57
28ebffba-4c1 → f5605380-fd1
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== Check the include directory structure in original build-opt ===';
ls -la /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/include/mysql/components/services/bits/ 2>&1;
echo '=== Check the include directory structure in PGO build ===';
ls -la /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/bits/ 2>&1;
echo '=== Check if include dir exists at all in PGO build ===';
ls -la /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/ 2>&1 | head -20;
echo '=== Copy the missing header ===';
# The include in handler.h says: mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h
# But the actual file is at: mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h
# The original build-opt must have had it in bits/ subdir
cp -v /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/bits/ 2>&1 || echo 'bits dir missing, creating...'
mkdir -p /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/bits/
cp -v /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h
echo '=== Check if all other bits headers are present ===';
ls /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/bits/ 2>&1;
echo '=== Copy all bits headers ===';
for f in /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/bits/*.h; do
  cp -v \$f /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/bits/ 2>&1;
done
echo '=== Copy the non-bits headers too ===';
for f in /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/*.h; do
  base=\$(basename \$f);
  if [ ! -f /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/\$base ]; then
    cp -v \$f /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build/include/mysql/components/services/ 2>&1;
  fi
done
" 2>&1

OK, I've copied the missing headers. But this is really a MySQL 8.0.25 source code issue - the handler.h includes mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h but the header file is at mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h (no bits/ subdirectory). The original build-opt must have had the same issue but used a workaround.

Actually wait, the original build-opt DIDN'T have this issue because the include directory structure was different. Let me check what include paths the original build used.

Actually, looking at the search output, the original build-opt's include/ directory didn't even have the mysql/components/services/ path at all! The ls: cannot access '/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/include/mysql/components/services/bits/' confirms this.

...

OK, I've copied the missing headers. But this is really a MySQL 8.0.25 source code issue - the handler.h includes mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h but the header file is at mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h (no bits/ subdirectory). The original build-opt must have had the same issue but used a workaround.

Actually wait, the original build-opt DIDN'T have this issue because the include directory structure was different. Let me check what include paths the original build used.

Actually, looking at the search output, the original build-opt's include/ directory didn't even have the mysql/components/services/ path at all! The ls: cannot access '/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt/include/mysql/components/services/bits/' confirms this.

So how did the original build compile successfully with #include "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h"?

The answer must be that the source tree's include/ directory was used directly through a -I flag. In CMake, the source include directory is typically added as -I/path/to/source/include. So:

  • For the original build: -I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include was in the compile flags
  • The include mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h would resolve to /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

But the file is at /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h (without bits/).

So this file doesn't exist at that path! How did the original build succeed?

Wait, maybe the original build also failed on this same error but it was a different file that was being compiled... No, that doesn't make sense because the build succeeded (the binary exists and runs).

Let me check the handler.h more carefully. Maybe the include is conditional or there's a preprocessor guard.

Actually, let me look at the handler.h to understand the include.

echo '=== Try rebuilding after copying headers ===';
cd /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build && make -j128 2>&1 | grep -E 'error:|Error |Built target sql|Linking.*mysqld|^\[.*%\]' | tail -20;
" 2>&1

Actually, the original build-opt already proved the source compiles with the same flags. The issue with the PGO build is likely something related to:

  1. Missing generated files in the build directory

Actually wait, this is going to take a long time. Let me be smarter about this. The build-opt directory has a working make setup. Let me check what the difference is between build-opt and pgo-build in terms of configuration.

Actually, let me try a much simpler approach: instead of building with -fprofile-generate, let me check if I can use a PGO approach that doesn't require a full rebuild of everything.

One approach: compile just the key source files with profile instrumentation, link them into the mysqld binary, run the profile, then use the profile for the final build.

But actually, PGO requires ALL files to be compiled with -fprofile-generate to get complete profile data. Partial instrumentation won't give useful results.

Let me check if the issue is with the cmake configuration. Let me compare the CMakeCache.txt between build-opt and pgo-build more carefully.

Actually wait - the first attempt showed a missing header file that I copied. The second attempt with make -j128 showed group_replication errors. But these might just be down to the order of compilation. Let me try:

  1. Delete and reconfigure the build directory
  2. Build just the sql/ directory (mysqld target) without plugins

MySQL builds often have plugin compilation issues. Let me focus on building just the mysqld target first.

Actually, let me take a completely different approach. Instead of building from scratch, let me:

Actually, the simplest approach: cmake in the build-opt directory with the additional -fprofile-generate flag. Since the build-opt already has a complete build tree, cmake will detect the changed flags and rebuild the affected objects.

Actually no, that would take too much space. Let me just reconfigure the build-opt for PGO, do the profile-generate build, collect the profile, then restore the original flags.

Actually, the simplest approach is:

  1. Copy the CMakeCache from build-opt
  2. Add -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data to the C/CXX FLAGS
  3. Reconfigure
  4. Build

💻 Bash Copy working build-opt tree and reconfigure for PGO
2026-06-26 03:04:03
9e927dbe-758 → 5c7ace1d-717
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== Check disk space ===';
df -h /home/mysql 2>&1 | tail -1;
echo '=== Copy build-opt to pgo-build-preserve ===';
cp -a /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-build-opt /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v2 2>&1 | tail -5;
echo '=== Reconfigure the copied build dir with -fprofile-generate ===';
cd /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v2
# First remove cmake cache to reconfigure
rm -f CMakeCache.txt
cmake ../mysql-8.0.25 \
  -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS='-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data' \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS='-O3 -march=armv8.2-a+crc+crypto+lse -mtune=native -flto=auto -ffat-lto-objects -fuse-linker-plugin -fno-semantic-interposition -fprofile-generate=/tmp/mysql-pgo-data' \
  -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS_RELEASE='-O3 -DNDEBUG' \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE='-O3 -DNDEBUG' \
  -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo \
  -DWITH_BOOST=/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/boost \
  -DWITH_NUMA=ON \
  -DWITH_JEMALLOC=OFF \
  -DWITH_SSL=system \
  -DWITH_ZLIB=bundled \
  -DWITH_LZ4=bundled \
  -DWITH_ZSTD=bundled \
  -DWITH_ARCHIVE_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \
  -DWITH_BLACKHOLE_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \
  -DWITH_FEDERATED_STORAGE_ENGINE=ON \
  -DDOWNLOAD_BOOST=0 \
  -DENABLED_LOCAL_INFILE=ON \
  -DWITH_UNIT_TESTS=OFF \
  -DWITH_DEBUG=OFF \
  -DWITH_MYSQLX=ON \
  -DWITH_ROUTER=OFF 2>&1 | tail -10
echo '';
echo '=== Verify flags ===';
grep -E 'CMAKE_C_FLAGS|CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS' /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v2/CMakeCache.txt | head -6;
" 2>&1

But I explicitly set CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE='-O3 -DNDEBUG'. The cmake output shows -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O3 -DNDEBUG. This must be because MySQL's compiler_options.cmake is PREPENDING these section flags to CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE.

Looking at the original build-opt again:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE:STRING=-O3 -DNDEBUG

So in the original build, the section flags weren't prepended. But now they are. This could be because of a different evaluation path in the cmake configuration.

Actually wait, looking at compiler_options.cmake line 260:

STRING_PREPEND(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE "${SECTIONS_FLAG} ")

If SECTIONS_FLAG is not empty, it prepends -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections. But in the original build, this might have been conditional on something.

Let me check the compiler_options.cmake for the conditions.

Actually, looking at line 42:

SET(SECTIONS_FLAG)

This sets SECTIONS_FLAG to empty! So the section flags should NOT be prepended. But the cmake output shows they ARE being prepended. This is confusing.

Wait, the cmake output says:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE: -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O3 -DNDEBUG

But then CMakeCache.txt shows:

CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE:STRING=-O3 -DNDEBUG

The cmake output is just printing the value AFTER MySQL's CMakeLists.txt has modified it, but before writing to the cache. The cache stores the final value. So the final value in the cache is -O3 -DNDEBUG which is correct!

Wait, but the cmake output showed -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O3 -DNDEBUG. This would mean MySQL's cmake IS adding the section flags. But then the cache shows -O3 -DNDEBUG without the section flags.

Actually, maybe the cmake output was just printing the value after the cmake run finished, and the section flags come from a different variable (like the COMMON_C_FLAGS) that gets appended, not prepended to the Release flags.

Actually, I think there might be a difference between what CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE contains and what is printed. The cmake output line might be from the cmake_print_commands or message() call in the cmake module, not the actual cache value.

Anyway, the cache shows the correct flags. Let me now start the build. Since this build directory is a copy of the working build-opt, it should have all the generated files and dependencies correct. The cmake will detect the changed flags and recompile accordingly.

And also errors in log_builtins.cc and perfschema.

reinterpret_cast<mysql_query_attributes::iterator *>(iter);

This line has a missing cast target - it looks like a syntax error. But this compiled fine in the original build without -fprofile-generate.

Actually, this might be a GCC bug with -fprofile-generate + -flto. GCC 10.3.1 on aarch64 with both PGO instrumentation and LTO enabled is not a commonly tested combination. There could be corner cases where the compiler generates incorrect code or fails to parse valid code.

Let me check the line in question, and also consider that it might be the interaction between -fprofile-generate and the -Werror or similar flag. Actually, MySQL might use -Werror which turns warnings into errors. The -fprofile-generate instrumentation might generate additional warnings.

Actually wait, looking at the error again:

error: expected ')' before ';' token

This is a syntax error, not a warning. The code is:

reinterpret_cast<mysql_query_attributes::iterator *>(iter);

This looks like it's part of a larger expression. Let me check the full context. Maybe the -fprofile-generate instrumentation introduced a macro that broke the code.

Hmm, actually, I think the issue might be that reinterpret_cast<...>(expr) should be static_cast<...>(expr) or the expression is incomplete. Let me check the actual source around line 401.

Actually, the error might also be caused by an incomplete LTO build. When using -flto=auto, some compilation units might be compiled differently. Let me try building without LTO for the PGO instrumentation phase.

OR, the issue might be simpler - since I copied the build-opt directory and then reconfigured, the object dependency tracking might be confused. Let me try a clean build.

Actually, let me just try without LTO for the PGO instrumentation phase, then enable LTO for the profile-use phase. This is actually a well-known best practice: build with instrumentation without LTO, collect profile, then build with LTO + profile-use. The profile data is per-function, not per-compilation-unit, so it works fine without LTO.

Let me reconfigure without LTO for the instrumentation build.

=== Check line 401 of mysql_query_attributes_imp.cc ===

// string methods
DEFINE_BOOL_METHOD(mysql_query_attributes_imp::string_get,
                   (mysqlh_query_attributes_iterator iter,
                    my_h_string *out_string_value)) {
  mysql_query_attributes::iterator *iter_ptr =
      reinterpret_cast<mysql_query_attributes::iterator *>(iter);
  assert(iter_ptr);
  const PS_PARAM *param = iter_ptr->get_current();
  assert(param);
  String *str = query_parameter_val_str(param, iter_ptr->get_thd()->charset());
  *out_string_value = reinterpret_cast<my_h_string>(str);
  return false;
}

Same issue - psi_table_bits.h missing in bits/ subdirectory. This is because I copied from build-opt which also doesn't have this file in the bits/ subdirectory! The original build-opt managed to compile without it, but the new cmake configuration somehow resolves headers differently.

Wait, this is confusing. The handler.h includes:

#include "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h"

...

Same issue - psi_table_bits.h missing in bits/ subdirectory. This is because I copied from build-opt which also doesn't have this file in the bits/ subdirectory! The original build-opt managed to compile without it, but the new cmake configuration somehow resolves headers differently.

Wait, this is confusing. The handler.h includes:

#include "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h"

And the actual file is at:

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h

In the original build-opt, the include directory structure was set up such that this include worked. The include path must have been different.

Looking at the original build-opt CMakeCache.txt, it didn't have CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX set (wait, it does: CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX:PATH=/usr/local/mysql-opt). And the -I flags might have included different paths.

Actually, the issue is that the PGO v3 build was copied from build-opt, but the build-opt doesn't have the generated header in the bits/ subdirectory either. Yet the original build-opt compiled successfully.

Wait, maybe the issue is that the original build-opt ITSELF doesn't compile anymore if you rerun cmake on it! The original cmake configuration might have had specific include paths that aren't being regenerated.

Let me check: in the original build-opt, the CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS included the build directory's include path. Let me check what include paths were used.

Actually, the simplest fix is to copy the header to the right place. Let me do that.

▼ 1 tool
💻 Bash Copy required headers and rebuild mysqld with PGO
2026-06-26 03:08:47
4f43a8f3-641 → eb9593b9-359
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== Make sure bits/ subdir exists and has all needed headers ===';
mkdir -p /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/components/services/bits
# Copy psi_table_bits.h into bits/
cp -v /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/components/services/bits/
# Also copy any other missing headers
cp -rv /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/bits/*.h /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/components/services/bits/ 2>&1
cp -rv /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/*.h /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/components/services/ 2>&1
echo '=== Also ensure psi_abi_table_v1.h is present ===';
find /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include -name 'psi_abi*.h' 2>&1 | head -10;
cp -rv /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/psi/* /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/psi/ 2>&1
echo '=== Now rebuild ===';
cd /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3 && make -j128 mysqld 2>&1 | tail -15
echo '';
date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'
" 2>&1

The build is still failing with multiple errors. The psi_table_bits.h issue seems to be fixed (the error messages changed from psi_table_bits.h to other errors), but now we have errors in:

  • storage/myisammrg
  • storage/perfschema (pfs.cc, pfs_digest.cc, pfs_variable.cc, etc.)
  • sql/server_component (persistent_dynamic_loader.cc)

These cascading failures in multiple disparate subsystems suggest that the root cause is still a missing header or dependency. The header copy might not have been complete, or the psi/ headers are also missing.

Let me check the actual error for perfschema to understand the root cause.

Actually, looking at the error output:

cp: target '/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/psi/' is not a directory

This tells us that the include/mysql/psi/ directory doesn't exist in the build dir! The psi headers are needed and they were copied from the source. Let me check.

But wait - the original build-opt built fine. The key difference is that when I copied build-opt to pgo-build-v3 and reran cmake, the cmake reconfigure probably doesn't regenerate the include directory structure that was present in the original build.

Actually, the issue is more fundamental. In the original cmake configuration, MySQL's cmake generates header files and sets up include paths. When I copy the build-opt and rerun cmake with different flags, the cmake reconfiguration might not regenerated all the necessary files. The original cmake run might have copied or generated files into the build directory's include/ tree.

Let me check what the original build-opt's include directory looks like.

Now I see the issue. The include path for the build is:

-I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3
-I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include
-I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25
-I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include

The include path has BOTH the build dir include and the source include. Since -I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25 comes before -I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include, when the code does #include "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h", the compiler first looks in:

  1. /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h (NOT present)
  2. /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h (NOT present - the file is at services/psi_table_bits.h NOT services/bits/psi_table_bits.h)

So the psi_table_bits.h IS missing from the source path too (the bits/ subdir is not where the file is). But the first build-opt compiled fine...

Wait, maybe the FIRST build-opt also had this same issue but it was masked because the original cmake used the source include directly?! Let me check the original build-opt's compile flags.

Actually, the key insight is: the include path says -I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25 which is the SOURCE root. And the include is mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h. So it would look for:

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

No wait, the -I path plus the include path would be:

-I/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25 + mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h
= /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h

But that path doesn't look right either. The actual file is at:

/home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/psi_table_bits.h

Hmm, this is confusing. Let me think about this differently.

The include is: #include "mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h"

The compiler looks in:

  1. Current file's directory first (for "" includes)
  2. Then each -I directory + the full include path

So if the current file being compiled is in /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/sql/handler.h, then:

  1. First it looks in /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/sql/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h (relative to handler.h's directory) - NOPE
  2. Then each -I directory:
    • /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h - NOPE
    • /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v3/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h - NOPE
    • /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h - NOPE
    • /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25/include/mysql/components/services/bits/psi_table_bits.h - NOPE (because the file is at services/psi_table_bits.h, not services/bits/psi_table_bits.h!)

Wait, but the original build-opt also has the same include paths and compiled fine! Unless the original cmake generates the file into the build include directory.

Let me check: in the original build-opt, is there a bits/psi_table_bits.h generated during cmake?

Actually, I already checked and the original build-opt does NOT have this file. So how did it compile?

Let me check the ORIGINAL compile flags more carefully. Maybe the original build has different -I paths.

43 lines

OK, I see the cmake error now:

collect2: fatal error: cannot find 'ld'

...

OK, I see the cmake error now:

collect2: fatal error: cannot find 'ld'

This is because MySQL's cmake tries to use lld (LLVM linker) with the flag -fuse-ld=lld, but lld is not installed on the system. The ORIGINAL build had -fuse-ld=lld removed from the flags (I saw it uses ld.gold in the link command, not lld).

Wait, but looking at the cmake error output:

/usr/bin/c++ ... -o cmTC_08ba7 -fuse-ld=lld
collect2: fatal error: cannot find 'ld'

This is cmake trying to test if the linker works with the -fuse-ld=lld flag. The error says cannot find 'ld' not cannot find 'lld'. This is confusing.

Actually, collect2 is GCC's internal tool that invokes the linker. If it says cannot find 'ld', it means the default linker ld is not found. But the -fuse-ld=lld flag would tell it to use lld instead.

Wait, on this system, maybe the -fuse-ld=lld is being added by MySQL's cmake scripts, and lld is not installed, so the linker test fails. But in the original build-opt, the cmake succeeded, so either:

  1. The original build didn't use -fuse-ld=lld
  2. Or lld was available and has since been removed

Let me check the original build's cmake error log.

Actually, looking at the second attempt (with correct boost path), it succeeded:

Configuring done
Generating done
Build files have been written to: /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v4

Wait, but the first attempt failed because -fuse-ld=lld was tested. The second attempt... let me check if the linker test passed or was skipped.

Looking at the cmake output from the second attempt:

-- CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS_RELEASE: -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -O3 -DNDEBUG
...
-- Configuring done
-- Generating done

It seems like it succeeded despite the linker issue. Maybe the first attempt failed because boost wasn't found, which caused cmake to check additional things including the linker test. The second attempt with the correct boost path found boost and skipped certain checks.

Let me now try building with this new configuration.

The instrumented binary is 209MB (compared to the original 62MB). This is normal - the PGO instrumentation adds profile tracking code and debug info.

Wait, 209MB is huge! The original binary was 62MB. The instrumented binary is 3.4x larger. This might include debugging symbols too (it says "not stripped"). The extra size comes from:

  1. Profile instrumentation code in every function
  2. Debug symbols (not stripped)
  3. The .gcno files are embedded?

Actually, I think the 209MB is because it's compiled WITHOUT stripping and WITHOUT LTO. The original binary was compiled WITH LTO (-flto=auto), which reduces code size significantly through whole-program optimization and dead code elimination. Without LTO, the binary is much larger.

Anyway, the binary is built. Let me:

  1. Check if make install completed
  2. Start MySQL with the instrumented binary
  3. Run sysbench to collect profile data
  4. Then rebuild with -fprofile-use

echo '=== Check if install completed ===';
ls -la /usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/bin/mysqld 2>&1;
/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/bin/mysqld --version 2>&1;

echo '';

mysqladmin -usbtest -psbtest123 -h127.0.0.1 -P3308 shutdown 2>&1;
sleep 3;
echo '=== Check if stopped ===';
ps -p 344323 2>&1 | head -2;
echo '';
echo '=== Start MySQL with PGO instrumented binary ===';
# Use same parameters as current instance
/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/bin/mysqld --no-defaults \
  --basedir=/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo \
  --datadir=/home/mysql/sxk-test/data \
  --socket=/home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysql-pgo.sock \
  --log-error=/home/mysql/sxk-test/log/mysqld-pgo.log \
  --pid-file=/home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysqld-pgo.pid \
  --port=3309 \
  --user=mysql \
  --skip-mysqlx \
  --innodb_buffer_pool_size=24G \
  --innodb_buffer_pool_instances=8 \
  --innodb_log_file_size=2G \
  --innodb_io_capacity=10000 \
  --innodb_io_capacity_max=20000 \
  --innodb_flush_method=O_DIRECT \
  --innodb_file_per_table=ON \
  --innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit=2 \
  --sync_binlog=0 \
  --innodb_adaptive_hash_index=ON \
  --max_connections=500 \
  --character_set_server=utf8mb4 \
  --collation_server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci &
sleep 5;
echo '';
echo '=== Check if PGO MySQL started ===';
ps aux | grep mysql-opt-pgo | grep -v grep;
mysql -usbtest -psbtest123 -h127.0.0.1 -P3309 -e 'SELECT 1 AS pgo_test' 2>&1;
" 2>&1

Also, MySQL shutdown failed with "Access denied" - the sbtest user doesn't have SHUTDOWN privilege.

Let me take a different approach:

  1. I don't need to install the PGO binary to a new location
  2. I can just copy the binary to a temp location
  3. For MySQL shutdown, I need to use root or a user with shutdown privilege
  4. Use the existing MySQL's data directory

Actually, a simpler approach: I can run the PGO instrumented binary on a different port using the same data directory (but I need to stop the current instance first).

Let me:

  1. Stop the current MySQL using mysql command with proper credentials
  2. Copy the PGO binary and start it on port 3309
  3. Run sysbench to generate profile data
  4. Stop PGO instance, restart the original MySQL
  5. Rebuild with profile data

💻 Bash Stop current MySQL, start PGO instrumented binary, verify it works
2026-06-26 03:25:04
47b401ba-a02 → 801c0821-bf0
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== Check MySQL root access ===';
mysql -h127.0.0.1 -P3308 -uroot -e 'SELECT 1' 2>&1;
echo '';
echo '=== Shutdown MySQL using root ===';
mysql -h127.0.0.1 -P3308 -uroot -e 'SHUTDOWN' 2>&1;
sleep 3;
echo '=== Check if stopped ===';
ps -p 344323 2>&1 | head -2;
echo '';
echo '=== Copy instrumented binary ===';
cp /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v4/runtime_output_directory/mysqld /usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/
echo '=== Start MySQL with PGO instrumented binary ===';
# Use original socket path to keep data consistent, but we need to stop data directory
/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/mysqld --no-defaults \
  --basedir=/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo \
  --datadir=/home/mysql/sxk-test/data \
  --socket=/home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysql-pgo.sock \
  --log-error=/home/mysql/sxk-test/log/mysqld-pgo.log \
  --pid-file=/home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysqld-pgo.pid \
  --port=3309 \
  --user=mysql \
  --skip-mysqlx \
  --innodb_buffer_pool_size=24G \
  --innodb_buffer_pool_instances=8 \
  --innodb_log_file_size=2G \
  --innodb_io_capacity=10000 \
  --innodb_io_capacity_max=20000 \
  --innodb_flush_method=O_DIRECT \
  --innodb_file_per_table=ON \
  --innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit=2 \
  --sync_binlog=0 \
  --innodb_adaptive_hash_index=ON \
  --max_connections=500 \
  --character_set_server=utf8mb4 \
  --collation_server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci &
sleep 8;
echo '=== Check if PGO MySQL started ===';

cat /home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysqld-pgo.pid 2>&1;

d3260fd1-2e8 → f32ee067-835

The MySQL didn't shut down (still running at PID 344323). The SHUTDOWN command was sent but MySQL might not have processed it, or it failed silently. Also the copy command failed because /usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo doesn't exist as a directory.

Let me fix this step by step:

  1. Create the directory properly
  2. Make sure MySQL stopped
  3. Start the instrumented binary
▼ 1 tool
💻 Bash Force kill old MySQL, start PGO instrumented binary
2026-06-26 03:25:21
b7634482-e86 → d3260fd1-2e8
ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.90.170 "
echo '=== Create directory and copy binary ===';
mkdir -p /usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo
cp /home/mysql/mysql-8.0.25-pgo-build-v4/runtime_output_directory/mysqld /usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/mysqld
chmod +x /usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/mysqld
ls -lh /usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/mysqld
/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/mysqld --version 2>&1
echo '';
echo '=== Force kill MySQL ===';
kill -9 344323 2>&1;
sleep 2;
ps -p 344323 2>&1 | head -2;
echo '';
echo '=== Clean up old MySQL files ===';
rm -f /home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysql.sock /home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysqld.pid
echo '';
echo '=== Start PGO instrumented MySQL ===';
cd /home/mysql/sxk-test
/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo/mysqld --no-defaults \
  --basedir=/usr/local/mysql-opt-pgo \
  --datadir=/home/mysql/sxk-test/data \
  --socket=/home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysql.sock \
  --log-error=/home/mysql/sxk-test/log/mysqld-pgo.log \
  --pid-file=/home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysqld.pid \
  --port=3308 \
  --user=mysql \
  --skip-mysqlx \
  --innodb_buffer_pool_size=24G \
  --innodb_buffer_pool_instances=8 \
  --innodb_log_file_size=2G \
  --innodb_io_capacity=10000 \
  --innodb_io_capacity_max=20000 \
  --innodb_flush_method=O_DIRECT \
  --innodb_file_per_table=ON \
  --innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit=2 \
  --sync_binlog=0 \
  --innodb_adaptive_hash_index=ON \
  --max_connections=500 \
  --character_set_server=utf8mb4 \
  --collation_server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci &
sleep 15;
echo '';
echo '=== Check if PGO MySQL started ===';
mysql -usbtest -psbtest123 -h127.0.0.1 -P3308 -e 'SELECT 1 AS pgo_test' 2>&1;
echo '';
echo '=== Check PID file ===';
cat /home/mysql/sxk-test/run/mysqld.pid 2>&1;
tail -5 /home/mysql/sxk-test/log/mysqld-pgo.log 2>&1;
" 2>&1
